Human-in-the-Loop
Intelligent Digital Twin for an
Educational Robotic Assembly Training Cell

This website provides two tracks of learning modules designed to bring digital twin education into industrial and manufacturing engineering curricula — one for students who build the system, and one for students who use it.

Track A

Implementation Guide

3 modules for graduate students and capstone builders. Documents how to construct, synchronize, and calibrate a human-in-the-loop digital twin from scratch.

3 modules
Track B

Student Learning Modules

4 modules for any IE or MfgE student who uses the system. Teaches confidence interpretation, human-in-the-loop decision making, and scenario analysis.

4 modules
Erik Tanjuakio BS Industrial Engineering, MS Engineering Management Mohamed Awwad Faculty Advisor Aditya Chivate Faculty Advisor
🎓 Applied engineering university  ·  Industrial and Manufacturing Engineering
Project Abstract

What This Project Does

Robotic assembly systems are increasingly central to modern manufacturing, yet many tasks still require human judgment when a robot encounters visual uncertainty or unexpected conditions. This project develops a human-in-the-loop intelligent digital twin for a robotic assembly cell that treats human oversight as a first-class architectural feature rather than an emergency fallback.

A vision pipeline classifies objects in the workspace and produces a continuous confidence score. When that score falls below a calibrated threshold (approximately 0.60, tuned to the specific task and lighting conditions), the robot pauses and routes the decision to a human operator. The operator's response is logged, fed back to the robot, and stored as training data for future system improvement. The digital twin synchronizes the virtual and physical states in real time, enabling path planning, telemetry visualization, and remote observation of system behavior.

Research Foundation

Three Literature Gaps This Project Addresses

The educational modules are each grounded in a specific gap identified across approximately 30 peer-reviewed sources in digital twin education research.

Gap 1  ·  Gonzalez-Perez and Ramirez-Montoya (2022)

No prerequisite map or time investment has been documented for DT education

Education 4.0 frameworks describe desired competencies but not the knowledge structure, sequencing, or learning effort required to produce them. The Track A implementation guide answers this empirically: which domains, in what order, for how many hours, and at what depth of understanding.

Addressed by: Track A modules A1, A2, A3

Gap 2  ·  Eriksson et al. (2022)

Retrofit DT education is valid, but its internal structure has never been documented

Eriksson validated the retrofit approach as the most industrially authentic method for DT education but did not document its prerequisite requirements, time distribution, or failure modes in enough detail for replication. Track A completes that documentation from a real build.

Addressed by: Track A modules A1, A2, A3

Gap 3  ·  Hazrat et al. (2023)

Human-in-the-loop judgment has never been implemented as a deliberate educational mechanism

Hazrat identifies human-centric decision making as the critical Industry 5.0 competency: knowing when to trust automation and when to intervene. No prior DT system has built this competency into its architecture as a designed teaching feature. Module B3 is that system.

Addressed by: Track B modules B2, B3, B4
Expert Consultations

Insights From Practitioners

Three expert interviews were conducted to ground the module design in real institutional practice. Each consultation surfaced challenges and design principles that directly informed the project scope.

🔬 Digital manufacturing teaching lab director

Private R1 research university  ·  Cyber-physical manufacturing teaching lab

One interviewed teaching lab director runs a teaching lab with a FANUC arm mounted on a linear track and a UR5 cobot, alongside conveyors, a CNC machine, and a laser engraver. The system is used across three courses (one undergraduate, two graduate) covering manufacturing systems, intelligent automation, and integrated automation. The lab uses a phone case assembly as its demonstration product.

He noted that hardware vendors such as Festo, Siemens, and Rockwell Automation provide educational discount packages and that industry contacts can help institutions identify appropriate hardware combinations and costs, a resource directly relevant to replicating this project's setup at other institutions.

FANUC + UR5 cobot Teaching lab 3 courses Festo / Siemens / Rockwell

🏭 Industrial engineering lab director

Large public R1 research university  ·  Industrial engineering labs

One interviewed industrial engineering lab director is building a digital twin ecosystem where any student, from a freshman statistics course to a senior capstone, can interact with real machine data from a physical lab. His vision: an operator puts on VR goggles from thousands of miles away and walks through the shop floor in real time, seeing OEE data, machine states, and production queues as they exist at that moment.

He described a persistent challenge that directly parallels this project: robot manufacturers (including FANUC, Techman, and Epson) deliberately limit data access, making it difficult to extract real-time telemetry into a unified digital twin. His lab uses Node-RED as an integration layer across dissimilar robot systems and is intentionally introducing Yaskawa, UR, and Epson arms alongside three different vision systems to teach students how to make incompatible systems work together.

Remote VR observation Dissimilar robot systems Node-RED integration OEE and real-time telemetry Vision systems

⚙️ Smart learning factory educator

Large public R1 research university  ·  Smart learning factory

One interviewed smart learning factory educator has spent three years building digital twins for a seven-station smart factory that assembles skateboards: six cooperative cells each with a UR10e arm, plus one fully automated kitting station. He tried three approaches in sequence: Unity (functional for a single arm but labor-intensive and hard to generalize), AWS TwinMaker (good results but expensive when sampling rates are high), and Visual Components (most successful, obtained via educational partnership).

The most important structural insight for this project came directly from his practice: capstone students build the digital twin, and then separate classes use it for product design and production planning exercises. This is the exact rationale behind separating Track A (builders) from Track B (users). He also recommended ROS 2 as a foundation for any robotic operating system integration and noted that pushing simulated commands to physical PLCs remains a future milestone his team has not yet reached safely.

Visual Components Unity / AWS TwinMaker 7-station smart factory ROS 2 Build vs. use distinction
"You should be able to sit on a beach somewhere thousands of miles away, open up your laptop, put on your goggles, and be on our shop floor, and see exactly what every machine is doing, how it's operating, what its OEE is, uptime, downtime, good parts, bad parts."

Industrial engineering lab director, Large Public R1 Research University

"That digital twin is then usable by a different class for the design of something within that particular cooperative station."

Smart learning factory educator, Research-Intensive Engineering Technology Program  ·  the rationale behind separating Track A from Track B

Educational Deliverables

Two Tracks. One System. Different Audiences.

The core deliverable of this project is a set of seven learning modules organized into two tracks. They are designed to be used independently or together, depending on the course and the student's role. Select a track below to browse and open the modules.

Track A

Implementation Guide

For graduate students and capstone builders

Three modules that document how to build, synchronize, and calibrate a human-in-the-loop digital twin — from system architecture through vision pipeline and threshold calibration. Closes literature gaps on prerequisite structure and retrofit build documentation.

  • A1 System Architecture & Prerequisites
  • A2 Building & Synchronizing the Digital Twin
  • A3 Vision Pipeline, Confidence & HitL Logic
Track B

Student Learning Modules

For any IE or MfgE student who uses the system

Four modules that teach students how to interpret confidence scores, make human-in-the-loop decisions, and evaluate system performance through designed experiments. No build knowledge required. Closes the literature gap on HitL judgment as a deliberate educational competency.

  • B1 Understanding the Digital Twin
  • B2 Vision, Uncertainty & Confidence Scoring
  • B3 Human-in-the-Loop Decision Making Core
  • B4 Scenario Analysis & System Evaluation
Project Background

Context and Scope

📋 Project Type

  • Master's project in Engineering Management
  • IISE Annual Conference and Expo 2026
  • Primary track: Engineering Education
  • Secondary track: Modeling and Simulation
  • Keywords: Digital Twins, Human-in-the-Loop Robotics, Robotic Assembly, Engineering Training Systems, Real-Time Simulation

🔧 Physical System

  • Yaskawa HC10 collaborative robot arm
  • YRC1000 controller with Ethernet interface
  • Camera mounted above the workspace
  • Pick-and-place color sorting task
  • University automation lab

💻 Software Stack

  • RoboDK for digital twin simulation
  • OpenCV for HSV extraction and K-means clustering
  • Python for GMM confidence modeling
  • MATLAB Simulink for HitL state machine
  • ROS 2 for communication middleware

📚 Literature Foundation

  • Approximately 30 peer-reviewed sources synthesized
  • Covers DT architecture, IoT protocols, Industry 4.0 and 5.0
  • Robotic assembly, human-robot interaction, simulation platforms
  • DT-based pedagogy and learning outcomes
Home › Track A

Track A  ·  Implementation Guide

Documentation for graduate students and future builders. This track closes Gaps 1 and 2: it provides the empirical prerequisite map, time investment breakdown, and retrofit build documentation that have never existed in prior DT education research.

👤 Audience: Graduate students and capstone builders

What this track is for

These three modules document how the HitL Digital Twin system was built, in enough detail for a future graduate student to replicate or extend it. They answer questions Track B deliberately does not: what software is required, what breaks and why, how long each component actually takes, and what depth of understanding you reach. This is Gap 1 and Gap 2 closed from real build experience.

Track A

A1

System Architecture and Prerequisites

Entry point for all builders. Covers the five-subsystem architecture, an empirical prerequisite knowledge map with sequencing, estimated time investment per component (73 hours total documented), and an understanding depth framework for each domain. Includes the IoT data pipeline diagram and time investment chart.

3 to 4 hours No lab required 7 knowledge domains
Track A

A2

Building and Synchronizing the Digital Twin

Step-by-step RoboDK model construction, HC10 kinematic verification, and ROS 2 synchronization loop implementation targeting 20 Hz and under 50 ms latency. Includes a full retrofit build log documenting 19 hours across five tasks with root causes identified for each major time driver.

12 to 15 hours Lab required 5 build tasks documented
Track A

A3

Vision Pipeline, Confidence Calibration and HitL Logic

The core technical build: OpenCV HSV extraction, K-means clustering, GMM confidence scoring, threshold calibration protocol using a 200-event empirical method, and the HitL state machine. Documents the intervention log schema that makes Track B data analysis possible. Includes confidence distribution and decision flow diagrams.

10 to 14 hours Lab required 3-stage pipeline
Recommended sequence: Start with A1 to understand the full architecture before touching any hardware. A2 assumes A1 is complete; the validation protocol depends on knowing what synchronization fidelity thresholds mean. A3 assumes A2 is working; the threshold calibration is only meaningful when the synchronization loop is confirmed accurate. Do not skip the A2 validation protocol before starting A3.
Home › Track B

Track B  ·  Student Learning Modules

Modules for any IE or MfgE student who uses the digital twin, not for students who build it. These modules close Gap 3: they teach human-in-the-loop judgment as a designed educational competency, not an incidental outcome.

🎓 Audience: IE and MfgE undergraduate and graduate students

What this track is for

These four modules assume the digital twin is already built and running. Students walk in, the system is operating, and their task is to understand it, stress-test it, and make a reasoned judgment about whether human oversight improves outcomes. This mirrors the structure Smart learning factory educator described at Purdue: capstone students build, then different classes use the result for product design and analysis. The modules progress from conceptual (B1, no lab needed) to hands-on investigation (B2 and B3) to open-ended capstone analysis (B4).

Track B

B1

Understanding the Digital Twin

Conceptual foundation for all students. Distinguishes digital models, digital shadows, and true digital twins using Fuller et al. (2020). Places the system in Industry 4.0 and 5.0 context. Includes a three-run observation table for navigating the interface. No lab access required, making it suitable for large lecture cohorts.

75 minutes No lab required Conceptual foundation
Track B

B2

Vision, Uncertainty and Confidence Scoring

How the vision pipeline works and what drives the confidence score. Includes a pre-lab prediction activity for five conditions (baseline, occlusion, shadow, similar color, rotated object), a lab observation log, and a threshold tradeoff analysis. Students commit predictions before running trials, then compare results to outcomes.

90 minutes Lab required 5-condition experiment
CORE Track B

B3

Human-in-the-Loop Decision Making

The core module. Students become part of the robot's decision architecture. Includes a 20-trial baseline log and a 10-trial degraded conditions log, with post-lab analysis comparing student accuracy against the autonomous system's performance at sub-threshold confidence levels. Connects findings to Broad et al. (2017) and the Industry 5.0 competency Hazrat et al. (2023) identifies as missing from every prior DT curriculum.

90 to 120 minutes Lab required 30 logged trials
Track B

B4

Scenario Analysis and System Evaluation

Capstone module. Students design their own production scenario (routine throughput, degraded conditions, or an R1 lab-style rush order), collect 15-trial autonomous and 15-trial HitL data, complete a five-metric comparison table, and write a 300-word evidence-based argument about HitL deployment. Includes the architecture diagram and confidence distribution for in-session reference.

2 hours lab, 1 hour analysis Lab required Student-designed scenario
Sequence and prerequisite notes: B1 can stand alone and requires no lab access; it works for large lectures or as pre-lab reading. B2 and B3 should be done in order; B2 builds the vocabulary for B3's data analysis. B4 works best after the full B1 through B3 sequence and serves as a culminating assessment. Students do not need any knowledge of how the digital twin was built. Track A and Track B are entirely independent.
Home › Track A › Module A1
Track A — Implementation Guide

Module A1: System Architecture & Prerequisites

👤 Graduate / future builders ⏱ 3–4 hours reading

⚙ Requirements & Components

Access

  • No hardware required
  • Repository or project documentation

Software

  • Robot simulation platform with API access
  • Python 3.8 or later
  • Robotics middleware (e.g., ROS 2 or equivalent)

Prior knowledge

  • Introductory robotics or automation concepts
  • Basic scripting in Python or similar language

This module is the entry point for anyone building or extending the Human-in-the-Loop Intelligent Digital Twin developed in a university automation lab. It documents what the system is, what prior knowledge is required, and what depth of understanding each component produces. This is the empirical answer to the gap identified by González-Pérez & Ramírez-Montoya (2022): no prior retrofit DT project documented its prerequisite structure, time investment, or understanding depth.

Learning objectives

  • Describe the full HitL DT system architecture from sensor to operator interface
  • Identify prerequisite knowledge domains and their correct sequencing
  • Estimate realistic time investment per system component
  • Distinguish deep, moderate, and surface-level understanding for each domain
  • Navigate the repository structure and locate relevant codebases

System Architecture

The HitL Intelligent Digital Twin is composed of five interconnected subsystems. Understanding the boundary between each subsystem and how data crosses those boundaries is more important than deep expertise in any single area at the outset.

Subsystem 1: Physical robot (HC10 + YRC1000)

The Yaskawa HC10 is a six-DOF collaborative robot controlled by the YRC1000 controller. State data — joint angles, end-effector pose, gripper state — is exported via Ethernet. What the system does not expose by default: force-torque data requires additional hardware, and high-frequency joint state streaming requires controller-side configuration.

Subsystem 2: Vision pipeline (camera + OpenCV)

A camera mounted above the workspace captures the assembly area. OpenCV performs HSV color extraction and K-means clustering to classify objects. The output is a continuous confidence score between 0 and 1 derived from the centroid distance of the classification cluster.

Subsystem 3: Confidence engine and threshold logic

The confidence engine compares the score to a calibrated threshold (approximately 0.60, tuned to the task and lighting conditions). Above threshold: the robot acts autonomously. Below threshold: the robot pauses and routes the decision to an operator.

Subsystem 4: RoboDK digital twin

RoboDK maintains a real-time kinematic model of the HC10. State updates from the YRC1000 are pushed to the RoboDK API, which updates the virtual robot joint configuration. Path plans created in RoboDK are executed on the physical robot.

Subsystem 5: Human interface (GUI / LLM)

When confidence falls below threshold, the operator interface displays the camera view with the candidate object highlighted, the confidence score, and the system best guess. Responses via GUI or LLM are timestamped and logged.

Prerequisite Knowledge Map

The table below is the empirical prerequisite map that González-Pérez & Ramírez-Montoya (2022) identified as missing from every prior DT education study.

Knowledge domainSequenceEst. hoursUnderstanding depth achieved
Python + ROS 2 basics1st8 hDeep — required to debug every downstream component
RoboDK API and kinematics2nd12 hDeep — model accuracy determines DT fidelity
YRC1000 Ethernet interface3rd15 hModerate — protocol understood; controller internals opaque
OpenCV and color segmentation4th10 hDeep — pipeline fully transparent and customizable
GMM confidence modeling5th8 hModerate — concepts applied; not derived from first principles
HitL logic and threshold design6th6 hDeep — core contribution; must be owned fully
Sync and integration testing7th14 hModerate — functional understanding; edge cases remain

Repository Structure

  • /physical — HC10 control scripts, YRC1000 configuration, ROS 2 launch files
  • /vision — OpenCV pipeline, K-means and GMM scripts, confidence calibration notebooks
  • /digital_twin — RoboDK model, API interface, state synchronization loop
  • /modules — Educational module documents (Track A and Track B)

Reflect before proceeding to Module A2

  • Draw the data flow from the physical robot to the RoboDK visualization. What data crosses each boundary, and at what rate?
  • The YRC1000 interface took approximately 15 hours to understand. What specific aspects contributed most?
  • The confidence threshold is approximately 0.60. What information would you need to argue for raising or lowering this value?
  • Which prerequisite domains do you have deep understanding of already? Which will require the most preparation?

References: González-Pérez & Ramírez-Montoya (2022); Lee et al. (2015); Fuller et al. (2020); Eriksson et al. (2022)

Home › Track A › Module A2
Track A — Implementation Guide

Module A2: Building & Synchronizing the Digital Twin

👤 Graduate / future builders ⏱ 12–15 hours hands-on build

⚙ Requirements & Components

Hardware

  • Robot arm with network-accessible controller
  • Compute node connected to robot controller over a local network

Software

  • Robot simulation and path planning platform
  • Robot driver compatible with your arm and middleware
  • Robotics middleware for data streaming

Prerequisites

  • Module A1 completed
  • Familiarity with command-line and network configuration

This module walks through building the RoboDK digital twin and synchronizing it with the physical HC10 in real time. It addresses Eriksson et al. (2022) Gap 2: that retrofit DT education is valid but has never been documented with enough granularity for replication.

Learning objectives

  • Build a functional HC10 kinematic model in RoboDK with accurate joint limits
  • Establish a real-time state synchronization loop between the YRC1000 and RoboDK API
  • Implement the ROS 2 data pipeline from joint state topics to the DT state layer
  • Characterize the synchronization latency of the completed system
  • Document the three most common failure modes and their resolutions

System Synchronization Architecture

Part 1: RoboDK Model Construction

Step 1 — Load the HC10 from the RoboDK library

File → Open Online Library → search "HC10". Drag into your station. Verify joint limits against the HC10 datasheet — the community model may have inaccuracies in the wrist joints. Correct these before proceeding.

Step 2 — Define the workspace reference frame

Create a reference frame at the physical robot base. Measure the offset between the robot base and camera mounting point. Any error here propagates through every downstream operation.

Step 3 — Configure the TCP (Tool Center Point)

Mount the gripper in RoboDK and define the TCP offset accurately. For the current gripper, the TCP is at the centroid of the jaw contact surface. Verify by jogging the physical robot to a known position and comparing the DT display.

Step 4 — Verify kinematic accuracy

Run tools/verify_kinematics.py to command five known poses and compare DT-predicted TCP position against measured physical position. Target: less than 3 mm error. Document your calibration error before proceeding.

Part 2: State Synchronization Loop

Implementation steps

  1. Install motoman_ros2 driver and verify /joint_states publishes at target frequency
  2. Create sync_node.py in /digital_twin/src/ using the repository template
  3. Test at slow robot speeds first — verify DT tracks physical motion with no visible lag
  4. Stress-test at maximum joint velocity — document dropped frames or lag spikes
  5. Log achieved synchronization latency as your fidelity metric (target: less than 50 ms end-to-end)

Common failure modes (documented from build experience)

  • Joint state timestamps drift if robot controller and compute node clocks are unsynchronized — use chrony or PTP on both systems
  • RoboDK API calls block the sync loop when the DT renders complex scenes — reduce rendering quality during high-frequency sync
  • The HC10 driver drops connections after extended idle — add a reconnect handler with exponential backoff
  • Joint angle unit mismatch (radians vs degrees) between ROS driver and RoboDK API is a silent failure — verify with a single-joint motion first

Part 3: Validation Protocol

  • Static accuracy: 5 known joint configurations — DT matches physical to within 3 mm TCP error
  • Dynamic accuracy: continuous path at 30% max speed — DT maintains visual synchronization
  • Latency: measured end-to-end sync delay less than 50 ms at 20 Hz
  • Recovery: simulate network interruption — DT reconnects within 5 seconds
  • Long-duration: 30-minute continuous operation — no memory leaks or performance degradation

Retrofit Build Log — Gap 2 Documentation

TaskTimePrimary time driver
HC10 RoboDK model and joint limit verification4 hInaccuracies in community model; manual correction required
Reference frame calibration3 hPhysical measurement precision; iterative correction
ROS 2 driver installation and clock sync5 hmotoman_ros2 build dependencies; chrony configuration
Sync loop implementation4 hAPI blocking issue; rendering quality adjustment
Validation protocol execution3 hLatency source identification; recovery testing

Reflect before proceeding to Module A3

  • Your sync loop achieves 18 Hz instead of 20 Hz. Is this acceptable? What would you change?
  • The DT shows accurate TCP position but joint angles look wrong. What does this tell you about the calibration issue?
  • A colleague suggests skipping the 30-minute long-duration test. What argument do you make for including it?
  • Document your own calibration error. How does it compare to the 3 mm target?

References: Eriksson et al. (2022); Quigley et al. (2009); Koenig & Howard (2004); Rasheed et al. (2020); Fuller et al. (2020)

Home › Track A › Module A3
Track A — Implementation Guide

Module A3: Vision Pipeline, Confidence Calibration & HitL Logic

👤 Graduate / future builders ⏱ 10–14 hours build + calibration

⚙ Requirements & Components

Hardware

  • Camera or imaging sensor positioned above the workspace
  • Physical objects representing at least two distinct categories
  • Functioning robot and digital twin sync from Module A2

Software

  • Computer vision library (e.g., OpenCV or equivalent)
  • Statistical classification library for confidence modeling
  • Control logic environment for HitL state machine

Prerequisites

  • Module A2 completed and validated
  • Labeled training examples for each object category

This module covers the OpenCV vision pipeline, GMM-based confidence scoring, threshold calibration, and the HitL state machine. This is the core technical contribution and directly closes Hazrat et al. (2023) Gap 3: implementing uncertainty-aware human intervention as a designed architectural feature.

Learning objectives

  • Implement HSV color extraction and K-means clustering for object classification
  • Build and calibrate a GMM-based confidence scoring model
  • Calibrate the confidence threshold using empirical performance data
  • Implement the HitL state machine routing below-threshold decisions to operators
  • Log operator responses and connect them to the robot execution pipeline

Part 1: Vision Pipeline

Stage 1 — HSV color extraction

Convert camera frames from BGR to HSV. HSV separates hue (color identity) from value (brightness), making classification more robust to lighting variation. Define HSV ranges for each object category under your specific lab lighting — do not use generic values from tutorials, as lighting conditions dominate the result.

Stage 2 — K-means classification

Apply K-means with k equal to the number of object categories. Confidence score formula: conf = 1 minus (d_min / d_max), where d_min is the distance to the nearest centroid and d_max is the maximum inter-centroid distance. This normalizes confidence to [0, 1].

Stage 3 — GMM refinement

K-means produces hard boundaries. A Gaussian Mixture Model trained on labeled observations produces soft probabilistic boundaries better suited to visually similar objects. Train with at least 50 labeled examples per category under your lab lighting.

Part 2: Threshold Calibration Protocol

  1. Collect 200 classification events — record confidence score and ground-truth outcome for each
  2. Plot confidence score vs. accuracy rate — identify the inflection where accuracy drops below your acceptable error rate (recommend 95%)
  3. Set the threshold at this inflection — approximately 0.60 for this pick-and-place task, though the value is task-specific and should be recalibrated for different conditions
  4. Validate with 50 additional events: measure (a) human intervention rate, (b) autonomous accuracy rate, (c) overall accuracy
  5. Document the calibration dataset — recalibrate if lighting or part geometry changes

Part 3: HitL State Machine

Three states: AUTONOMOUS (confidence above threshold, robot acts), PENDING_HUMAN (confidence below threshold, robot pauses, operator prompted), EXECUTING_CORRECTION (human input received, robot acts on corrected classification).

Operator interface requirements

  • Current camera frame with classification candidate highlighted
  • Confidence score and which threshold was breached
  • System best guess for the classification
  • Confirm / override / mark unclear options
  • Timestamp and log every response to the interventions database

Intervention log schema

  • timestamp — Unix timestamp of the intervention event
  • confidence_score — score at time of trigger
  • system_guess — autonomous system best classification
  • human_response — operator-provided classification
  • agreement — boolean: did human agree with system guess?
  • cycle_time_delta — additional time added by human review

What the log enables

  • Compute human intervention rate: interventions / total classifications
  • Compute autonomous accuracy at the threshold boundary
  • Analyze agreement rate: high agreement means threshold may be too conservative
  • Build a retraining dataset for the GMM using human-corrected labels — the system improves over time

Reflect before handing off to Track B users

  • Your calibration finds the threshold should be 0.72 rather than 0.60. What does this tell you about your lighting conditions?
  • After 500 interventions, human agreement with the system guess is 92%. What does this tell you about performance near the threshold?
  • A future user wants to add a fourth object category. Which steps must they repeat, and in what order?
  • Describe one improvement to the log schema that would make the Track B analysis modules more informative for students.

References: Hazrat et al. (2023); Jasim & Plapper (2014); Lenz et al. (2015); Broad et al. (2017); Amershi et al. (2014)

Home › Track B › Module B1
Track B — Student Learning Modules

Module B1: Understanding the Digital Twin

👤 Any IE or MfgE student — no lab access required ⏱ 75 minutes

⚙ Requirements & Components

Lab access

  • Not required — suitable for any classroom or lecture setting
  • Optional: view-only access to a running digital twin interface

Materials

  • Module document
  • Pen or digital notes for the observation table

Prerequisites

  • None — appropriate as an entry point for any IE or MfgE student

"Digital twin" is used to describe everything from a 3D rendering to a sophisticated real-time system. This module gives you a precise vocabulary and a way to evaluate whether any given system actually qualifies. By the end, you will be able to look at the lab system and explain exactly what makes it a true digital twin.

Learning objectives

  • Distinguish between digital models, digital shadows, and true digital twins
  • Explain the physical–digital synchronization relationship and why it matters
  • Place the system in the context of Industry 4.0 and Industry 5.0
  • Read and interpret live telemetry output from the digital twin interface
  • Articulate why the human-in-the-loop design is an Industry 5.0, not Industry 4.0, feature

Background: The Three Fidelity Levels

Fuller et al. (2020) provide the most useful precise definition by distinguishing three levels based on data flow:

Digital model: A virtual replica with no real-time connection to the physical system. A CAD file of the robot is a digital model. Changes to the physical system are not reflected.

Digital shadow: The physical system streams data to the virtual model one-way. You can observe the physical system through the virtual one but cannot use the virtual to drive the physical.

True digital twin: Bidirectional, real-time synchronization. The physical system updates the virtual model; the virtual model can generate commands back. Planning in the virtual environment and executing physically is only possible with a true digital twin.

The lab system is a true digital twin. Joint states from the the robot arm update the simulation model in real time. Path plans from the simulation environment are pushed to the the robot controller for physical execution.

Industry 4.0 and Industry 5.0 Context

Industry 4.0 built the infrastructure that makes digital twins possible: IoT sensors, cloud computing, real-time data pipelines. But it treated human operators primarily as fallbacks for system failures. The digital twin you will use embodies Industry 5.0 thinking: human judgment is a routine, valued contribution, not an emergency override.

Lab Activity: Navigating the Interface

Step 1 — Identify the four information panels

Find: (1) the 3D the simulation environment view of the virtual robot in real time; (2) the camera feed from the physical workspace; (3) the telemetry panel showing joint angles, TCP position, and gripper state; (4) the confidence gauge.

Step 2 — Observe and record three classification events

RunConf. scoreObject classifiedAutonomous or HitL?Outcome correct?

Step 3 — Assess synchronization fidelity

While the robot moves, compare the 3D DT view and the physical robot. Note any lag, joints that differ, or motion in one not reflected in the other.

Discussion questions

  • What would be different if this system were only a digital shadow? Give a concrete example of something you could no longer do.
  • Some advanced DT implementations aim to let an operator observe the full shop floor remotely in real time. What would need to be added to the lab system to get closer to that vision?
  • Which specific feature makes this system Industry 5.0 rather than Industry 4.0? Could you remove it and still call it a digital twin?
  • Name one thing the DT can tell you that you could not learn from watching the physical robot alone.

References: Fuller et al. (2020); Grieves & Vickers (2017); Xu et al. (2021); Breque et al. (2021)

Home › Track B › Module B2
Track B — Student Learning Modules

Module B2: Vision, Uncertainty & Confidence Scoring

👤 IE / MfgE students — lab access required ⏱ 90 minutes

⚙ Requirements & Components

Lab access

  • Running digital twin system with live camera and confidence display
  • Objects representing each test condition
  • Ability to introduce environmental changes (e.g., shadow, occlusion)

Lab support

  • Lab coordinator or instructor to introduce each condition

Prerequisites

  • Module B1 completed (recommended)
  • No build or implementation knowledge required

Automated systems often fail not because they encounter something unmanageable, but because they do not know they cannot manage it. A robot that misclassifies an object with wrong certainty causes more damage than one that pauses to ask. This module builds your understanding of how the vision system quantifies its own uncertainty.

Learning objectives

  • Explain how color-space color extraction and the clustering algorithm clustering produce a classification
  • Interpret a confidence score and explain what drives it higher or lower
  • Identify the physical conditions that push confidence below the intervention threshold
  • Predict, before running a trial, whether a scenario will trigger human intervention
  • Explain the tradeoff between setting the threshold higher vs. lower

How the Vision System Works

Stage 1: color-space color extraction

Each pixel is converted from RGB to color-space. color-space separates color identity (hue) from lighting effects (value), making classification more robust to ambient light variation. Significant lighting changes — a shadow, a different lamp — can shift an object's color-space values outside its trained range. This is the most common cause of sudden confidence degradation.

Stage 2: the clustering algorithm classification

Each detected object is characterized by its average color-space values and shape geometry. the clustering algorithm assigns it to the nearest cluster centroid. Confidence score: 1 minus the normalized distance from the observation to its nearest centroid. An observation on a centroid scores 1.0; one equidistant between two centroids scores near 0.5.

Stage 3: Threshold comparison

The confidence score is compared to the calibrated threshold (approximately 0.60). Above: robot proceeds autonomously. Below: system pauses and routes to an operator.

Lab Activity: Pre-lab Prediction

For each condition below, predict whether confidence will stay above 0.60 (autonomous) or drop below it (HitL triggered). Write your reasoning.

ConditionYour predictionReasoning
Normal conditions (baseline)
Object partially occluded
Shadow cast across workspace
Two objects of similar color
Object rotated 90° from training

Lab Observations

ConditionConf. scorePrediction correct?Notes on camera view
Baseline
Occlusion
Shadow
Similar color
Rotated 90°

Threshold Tradeoff Analysis

Threshold raised to 0.80

  • More human interventions: every borderline case is reviewed
  • Higher autonomous accuracy rate
  • Longer cycle times overall
  • Use when cost of errors is high (e.g., surgical assembly)

Threshold lowered to 0.40

  • Fewer interventions: more autonomous decisions
  • Lower autonomous accuracy rate
  • Faster cycle times overall
  • Use when cost of delay exceeds cost of occasional errors

Discussion questions

  • You ran the occlusion condition and confidence dropped to 0.31. Should you raise or lower the threshold in response? Explain why neither direction is automatically correct.
  • The system correctly classified an object with a confidence score of 0.42. A student argues the threshold is too high. How do you respond?
  • If this system were operated fully remotely, what challenges would that create for confidence monitoring that are not present when you are physically in the lab?
  • Name one application where you would set the threshold much higher than 0.60 and one where you would set it much lower. Justify both.

References: Lenz et al. (2015); Jasim & Plapper (2014); Collet et al. (2011); Rasheed et al. (2020)

Home › Track B › Module B3
Track B — Student Learning Modules

Module B3 (Core): Human-in-the-Loop Decision Making

👤 IE / MfgE students — lab access required ⏱ 90–120 minutes

⚙ Requirements & Components

Lab access

  • Running digital twin system with HitL interface enabled
  • Objects for baseline and degraded condition trials
  • Operator input method accessible to the student

Lab support

  • Lab coordinator to introduce degraded conditions for Part 2

Prerequisites

  • Modules B1 and B2 completed
  • No build or implementation knowledge required
This is the core module. The competency practiced here — knowing when to trust automation and intervening appropriately — is the central Industry 5.0 skill identified as absent from DT education by Hazrat et al. (2023).

You are about to become part of the robot's decision-making architecture. When vision confidence drops below the threshold, the robot stops and asks for your input. Your response directly controls what happens next. Your decisions will be logged, and you will analyze them afterward.

Learning objectives

  • Respond accurately and consistently to confidence-triggered intervention requests
  • Compare your decision accuracy to the autonomous system at the same confidence levels
  • Identify the types of cases where human judgment adds the most value
  • Explain shared autonomy and where it applies in Industry 5.0 manufacturing
  • Articulate what the logged data from your interventions could be used for beyond this exercise

Background: Shared Autonomy

Shared autonomy is the formal term for systems where control is dynamically split between a human and an autonomous agent based on context. Dragan & Srinivasa (2013) formalize this as a policy-blending framework: the autonomous policy and the human policy are combined, with the human's weight increasing as confidence decreases. In this lab the blending is a hard threshold — a deliberate simplification that makes the human role unambiguous and the data easy to analyze.

Broad et al. (2017) showed that systems can learn from human corrections: the more interventions are logged, the better the autonomous system can model what a human would do, requesting intervention less often over time. Your corrections today are training data for a future version of this system.

Part 1: Baseline Performance (20 trials)

What you will see when an intervention is triggered

  • The robot arm stops mid-operation — expected and intended
  • The operator panel displays the camera view with the object highlighted
  • The confidence score is shown (below threshold) along with the system's best guess
  • You may confirm, override, or mark as unclear
  • A timer records your response time
#Conf.System guessYour responseAgree?Time (s)

Part 2: Degraded Conditions (10 trials)

Condition introduced: _____________________________________________

#Conf.System guessYour responseAgree?Time (s)

Post-Lab Analysis

  • Accuracy rate Part 1 (%): ____
  • Accuracy rate Part 2 (%): ____
  • Agreement rate with system guess (%): ____
  • Average response time Part 1 (s): ____
  • Average response time Part 2 (s): ____

Post-lab writeup questions

  • What was your accuracy rate in Part 1? Did you outperform the autonomous system at that confidence level?
  • Did accuracy or response time change between Part 1 and Part 2? What does this reveal about human vs. machine performance under uncertainty?
  • In which cases did you disagree with the system's guess? What did you see in the camera view that the confidence score did not capture?
  • Broad et al. (2017) argue logged human corrections can train the autonomous system to improve. Based on your interventions, what would the system need to learn?
  • At what production volume would you start questioning whether HitL review remains economically justified?

References: Hazrat et al. (2023); Dragan & Srinivasa (2013); Broad et al. (2017); Amershi et al. (2014); Villani et al. (2018)

Home › Track B › Module B4
Track B — Student Learning Modules

Module B4: Scenario Analysis & System Evaluation

👤 IE / MfgE students — lab + data analysis ⏱ 2 hours lab + 1 hour analysis

⚙ Requirements & Components

Lab access

  • Running digital twin system with HitL interface enabled
  • Objects appropriate for your chosen scenario

Preparation

  • Scenario design form completed before arriving at the lab
  • Method for recording cycle times during trials

Prerequisites

  • Modules B1, B2, and B3 completed
  • Scenario hypothesis written in advance

This is the capstone module. You will design and run your own production scenario, measure system performance, and make a reasoned argument about whether the HitL architecture improved outcomes. You choose the scenario, you define the metrics, you draw the conclusions.

Learning objectives

  • Design a production scenario with defined start conditions and measurable success criteria
  • Run parallel autonomous and HitL trials and collect comparable performance data
  • Calculate and interpret key metrics: classification accuracy, intervention rate, and cycle time
  • Construct a reasoned argument for or against HitL deployment in your specific scenario
  • Connect your findings to the broader retrofit vs. build-from-scratch vs. platform-based DT debate

System Reference

Part 1: Scenario Design

Scenario name: _______________________________________________

Object types used: ___________________________________________

Special conditions introduced: ___________________________________________

Number of trials — autonomous: ___    HitL: ___

Primary metric: ________________

Hypothesis: _________________________________________

Scenario types from expert interviews

Type A: Routine throughput

  • Baseline conditions; no special challenges
  • Measure: accuracy and cycle time at steady state
  • Question: Is HitL cost justified when the system works well?
  • Source: practitioner interviews

Type B: Degraded conditions

  • Introduce a deliberate disruption
  • Measure: accuracy drop vs. HitL compensation
  • Question: At what severity does HitL pay off?
  • Source: practitioner interviews

Type C — Rush order / curve ball: Mid-run, introduce a new object type the system has not seen. Measure how the system responds and whether the run completes successfully. Source: Interviewed R1 lab educator — "What if we get a hot order that has to be done tomorrow?"

Part 2: Data Collection

Autonomous run (15 trials)

TrialConf. scoreCorrect?Cycle time (s)Notes

HitL run (15 trials)

TrialConf.Intervened?Correct?Cycle time (s)Notes

Part 3: Comparison Table

MetricAutonomous (your result)HitL (your result)
Classification accuracy (%)
Human intervention rate (%)
Average cycle time (s)
Total run time (s)
Errors requiring correction

Part 4: Final Argument (300 words)

"In your scenario, did the HitL architecture improve system performance? Under what conditions would you deploy it in a real factory, and at what production volume would you remove it?"

Connecting your findings to the literature

  • Hazrat et al. (2023): Does your data support their argument that human-centric decision-making is a critical workforce competency?
  • Eriksson et al. (2022): How does your experience using a retrofit DT compare to build-from-scratch approaches?
  • A persistent challenge in real manufacturing environments is making dissimilar systems work together. Did that challenge appear in your scenario?
  • One goal of a true digital twin is to let an operator understand the system state as if they were physically present. Did your scenario feel like that, or did physical proximity to the robot still matter?

References: Hazrat et al. (2023); Eriksson et al. (2022); González-Pérez & Ramírez-Montoya (2022); García et al. (2022); Broad et al. (2017)
